data.title: After Two Detrimental Earnings Events, Could AECOM Be Third Time Lucky?
data.description:
Introduction

AECOM (ACM) is a Dallas-based professional services business that is seen as a reliable authority in infrastructure consulting and advisory work (rendered mainly for global “public” clients that account for almost three-fourths of total business) in key end markets such as water, transportation, and facilities.

ACM’s underlying strength in offering fee-based knowledge-based services has been validated by ENR’s (Engineering News-Record) latest 2026 survey, which has seen it being ranked among the top two largest general architectural and engineering design firms in the world (among 500 candidates) for two years running.

Recent Earnings Events Have Been Destructive For ACM

Despite boasting such credentials within its industry, it’s fair to say that the investment community hasn’t quite warmed up to ACM for a while now. For instance, over the last 6 months, when ACM’s industrial and mid-cap peers have managed to generate positive returns of double digits, our stock in focus has eroded investor wealth by around -38%. Also, if you scrutinize the image below, you’ll note that much of the weakness over the past six months was triggered by developments in November 2025 and February 2026.

Is there a common denominator in those two months? Yes, indeed, and it essentially boils down to a weak performance by ACM during the earnings season.

Back in the third week of November 2025, after the company released its Q4 earnings (ACM follows a September-ending fiscal), the stock ended up declining for 4 straight days, contracting by -22% in aggregate during that period! The Q1 earnings in February weren't quite as bad, as the stock had initially gapped up following the earnings release the previous day (9th of February), but that initial euphoria proved to be short-lived, as the stock ended up the day lower and saw weakness persist for the next 3 days again, resulting in a -15% drawdown during that period!

Nonetheless, given how inimical earnings season has proven to be for ACM in the recent past, I wouldn’t be surprised to learn if its investors are biting their nails ahead of the next event, which is due in less than two weeks. To be more specific, ACM will release its Q2 2026 earnings on the 11th of May, following the close of the market, with a conference call due to be held the following day before the market opens. So, could Q2 2026 be more rewarding than the recent two quarters? Potentially, yes.

Upcoming Earnings- Main Talking Points

Firstly, investors should be aware that this is a business prone to seasonal shifts across quarters, and Q2 comes after what is traditionally the most challenging quarter for ACM, where harsher weather conditions, a lower number of working days (at least in Q1 2026), and the holiday season schedule ended up hampering the topline and overall productivity. However, do also note that Q2 is certainly not the strongest quarter for ACM, and conditions only meaningfully pick up in the second half of their fiscal year (recall that ACM subscribes to a September year-end). Having said all that, I would still expect ACM’s Q2 2026 topline performance to look good optically, because last year’s base is not bloated (revenue in Q2 2025 had declined by -4% YoY), making it easier to post solid YoY progress. Secondly, consensus expectations ($3.94B) too are on the lower side (having been trimmed by close to 1% over the past month), making it easier for ACM to facilitate a topline beat.

Then Q1 2026 was also keenly impacted by the 43-day government shutdown, a headwind that wasn’t present in Q2 2026, likely resulting in a higher cadence of awards (more so with the passage of all critical federal funding bills), which means even though strong revenue recognition is expected in Q2, one is also likely to see strong bookings, keeping the book-to-burn ratio above 1x, offering solid revenue visibility ahead.

While the private sector side of ACM’s business is not traditionally the largest (29% of the business mix), it has the potential to take on a larger share and drive ACM’s overall growth, given the pace of growth of their data center business, which is growing at a rate of 50% annually. ACM is not only involved in providing services linked to electrical engineering and infrastructure but also due diligence, testing, and feasibility work for hyperscalers.

Q2 will also be the first quarter where the market would have had decisive clarity on ACM’s construction management business, which was previously put up for a strategic review. While the backlog from this business is a plus, what’s also underrated is that it has a strong cash flow profile, which will enable ACM to push through its greater than 100% FCF conversion targets (FCF over net income) through FY29. Speaking of cash, while the real strength will come through in H2 2026, I expect Q2 to be much better than Q1, where the company was unable to generate any progress in free cash flow (YoY) despite a -30% YoY decline in CAPEX (because operating cash flow more than halved).

A Few Reasons To Consider ACM Stock Now

Firstly, it’s worth noting that ACM’s weakness in recent months could also make it more attractive for value-conscious investors. To better explain the valuation backdrop, I’ve also brought into consideration the other major publicly listed professional services and infrastructure consulting firms that operate in the end markets of water, environment, facilities, transportation, public sector, etc. The peer set includes the likes of Jacobs Solutions (J), Stantec (STN), Tetra Tech (TTEK), WSP Global (WSPOF), and Parsons Corp. (PSN).

Note that among all these stocks, ACM is priced at the lowest EBITDA multiple of just 10x, which represents a 15% discount to its peer set and also a 25% discount to the stock’s own rolling average.

I feel the market could be sleeping on ACM’s future potential because it has been fast-tracking AI in its business processes more rapidly than its peers (the benefits of which will be seen by way of faster delivery of design, higher productivity, reduced materials, and overall lower cost of infrastructure) and is poised to see strong operating leverage in the years ahead.

Some naysayers may suggest that ACM’s efforts to drive operating efficiency are just plain fluff, but it’s still worth remembering that this is a management team that has already showcased their ability to extract superior EBITDA from their employees (EBITDA per employee has grown at 1.6x the pace of revenue per employee over the past five years).

Nonetheless, I suspect ACM’s relatively cheap valuations at the current market price will also invigorate ACM’s management team to step up the pace of share repurchases even further. Investors may want to take note of the board-approved authorization that ACM management still has to buy back around $1.34B worth of its stock. This is not a small amount, accounting for 13% of the current market cap, and could help support the share price.

In keeping with the discussion on shareholder returns, also note that ACM is one of those names that tends to be very generous with the dividends it offers. The company has been growing its quarterly dividends every year since 2023, at an impressive CAGR of 20% (in contrast, most construction & engineering stocks don’t even grow their dividends, and of those that do, the growth rate has been much lower at 12-16% over the same duration), and this has also seen it make it to the Nasdaq US Rising Dividend Achievers Index [NURDAI], which covers around 70 US dividend growers that are poised to continue growing their dividends going forward. Investors who want to pursue dividend growers typically pursue the First Trust Rising Dividend Achievers (RDVY) ETF, which tracks NURDAI, or use this ETF as a reference to pursue potential dividend growers. I suspect those who resort to the latter will be interested to note that ACM’s current relative strength ratio versus other dividend growers is at six-year lows and is now trading at a 25% discount to its long-term mean of 1.45x. It is only a matter of time before that ratio mean-reverts to that long-term mean.

Then, if we look at ACM’s long-term monthly charts, we know that it has been in the midst of selling pressure since November 2025, but it appears that things may have gone overboard, and we could see a bottom soon enough. I say this because April’s monthly candle recently hit its lower Bollinger band (a measure of selling overextension, as it is two standard deviations below the 20-month rolling average), while the price is also now closer to levels last seen in 2023, when the stock had bottomed out. Investors may also want to note that the volumes associated with the selling over the past couple of months have been lower than what was seen before.

Conclusion

ACM, which appears to be out of favor, faces a key earnings event in less than two weeks from now. While Q2 earnings may not necessarily be the strongest in the year, we expect better trends from what was seen in Q1, while guidance too could potentially be lifted as the company enters its seasonally stronger H2. After prolonged weakness, we think that ACM may be worth a nibble now, as the stock looks oversold, offers a compelling shareholder return angle (both by way of potentially higher share repurchases as well as strong dividend growth), and is available at dirt-cheap EBITDA valuation multiples, which aren’t necessarily reflecting the expected operating leverage on offer. To conclude, we believe ACM would make for a good buy ahead of its earnings.
